text: Today we will be presenting a case of a robot-assisted partial nephrectomy in an ectopic pelvic kidney. Pelvic kidneys result from an incomplete or aberrant ascent during embryologic development. They are a rare occurrence and present special treatment challenges due to a greater risk of injury of aberrant vessels or abdominal viscera. The patient was a sixty-nine year old male who was referred for an incidental renal mass. He had no prior abdominal surgeries and had normal renal function. A CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a four point one centimeter mass in the lower pole suspicious for renal cell carcinoma in the left pelvic kidney as well as a duplication of the infrarenal IVC. A left renal artery arises off of the aortoiliac confluence. An accessory renal artery is seen arising off of the proximal left common iliac artery and traces along the lateral aspect of the kidney. Treatment options were discussed, and the patient elected to undergo a robot-assisted partial nephrectomy of this mass.

The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked, and robotic trochars are placed in the abdomen for pelvic surgery. The abdomen is entered using a varus needle technique, and the patient is placed in steep Trendelenburg to mobilize the bowels cranially. The kidney is identified in the pelvis. Blunt dissection and monopolar cautery are used to reflect the bowel and expose the kidney. Dissection is performed along the medial aspect of the kidney to identify the hilum, revealing the renal artery and vein in the cranial location, along with an additional renal artery from the common iliac. The vessels are carefully dissected for potential clamping.

Hemostasis is maintained using bipolar cautery and sponges. The surgeon exposes the renal mass by dissecting Girota's fascia, confirming its location with intraoperative ultrasound. The ureter is seen wrapping around the mass and is carefully skeletonized and mobilized medially. The kidney is mobilized, and the mass surface is fully exposed. Intraoperative ultrasound demarcates the limits between the mass and normal renal parenchyma. The surgeon clamps both renal arteries with curved bulldog clamps and excises the mass, identifying a plane between the tumor and normal parenchyma. A renal cyst is encountered, serving as a landmark for the mass limit based on preoperative imaging.

The surgeon excises the renal mass completely and ensures thorough hemostasis. The base of the resection is over-sewn with 2-0 monocryl suture, secured with Hemolock clips. After closing the initial layer, the clamps are removed, marking a total clamp time of eight minutes. The renal capsule is then closed with a 2-0 V-Lock suture, also secured with Hemolock clips after each throw.

The surgeon cuts both layers of sutures and removes the needles. The excised specimen is placed in an endocatch bag. The surgical bed is irrigated and examined for hemostasis, which is reinforced with hemostatic agents. Girota's fascia is closed with a 2-0 V-lock suture. The specimen is removed, and the surgical incisions are closed in layers.
The patient's post-operative course was uncomplicated and he was discharged home in stable condition on post-op day one. Pathology showed a four point five centimeter papillary renal cell carcinoma.

Robotic-assisted partial nephrectomy in patients with complex anatomy require adherence to the same basic surgical principles as any other surgery, including detailed preoperative planning of the optimal approach and port placements, meticulous dissection of hilar structures, and adequate exposure of the entirety of the mass. Some key steps that allowed us to successfully complete the surgery were to obtain appropriate preoperative imaging and the use of intraoperative ultrasound to complete sufficient mobilization of the kidney, which was achieved by optimal use of the fourth arm and the surgical assistant.
